forecasts.reasoning: The timeline to July 1, 2025 is very short (exactly two months). While the Supreme Court unanimously ordered the US government to facilitate Abrego Garcia's return, the executive branch has shown strong resistance, with President Trump explicitly stating he will not bring him back. Furthermore, El Salvador's President Bukele has publicly refused to release him from the CECOT mega-prison, calling the prospect 'preposterous'. This gives the US administration plausible deniability in court—they can claim they attempted to secure his release but cannot force a sovereign nation's hand. Even if a settlement or legal maneuver compels the administration to act, diplomatic realities and bureaucratic stalling can easily push any resolution past the July 1 deadline. Alternative solutions, such as removing him to a third country (Uganda/Liberia), are also being explored. Therefore, a return to US soil within the next 60 days is highly unlikely.

Based on the search results, negotiations between Vietnam and South Korea for the purchase of about 20 K9 self-propelled howitzers were in the "final stages" in January 2025. Crucially, Vietnamese troops had already completed a training program on the K9 system in South Korea in November 2024, and both sides had reportedly aligned on terms. From "final stages" in January 2025 to a deadline of July 1, 2025 gives roughly five months for formal announcement. The high level of commitment shown through troop training and alignment on terms strongly predicts an enacted deal within this timeframe. However, arms procurement negotiations can sometimes face bureaucratic delays or strategic hesitations, particularly given the sensitive geopolitical context for Vietnam, keeping the probability from being a near certainty. Therefore, I estimate an 81% chance of an announcement before the July 1, 2025 deadline.

The resolution requires the protest to have a start date AFTER the launch of this question (which is ≥ Jan 15, 2025, based on the background links). The massive protests following martial law began on Dec 3, 2024, so they do not qualify. To resolve YES, a distinctly new protest episode must start, visibly achieve 10,000+ participants, AND be added to the Carnegie Global Protest Tracker before July 1, 2025. While South Korea may have new large protests (such as election-related rallies or general strikes), the chances that Carnegie treats them as a mathematically discrete new entry and updates its tracker quickly enough (before July 1, 2025) is low. Think-tank trackers historically have lag times of several months to a year. Thus, the probability of fulfilling all criteria strictly before the deadline is quite low.

The time remaining until the resolution date (July 1, 2025) is extremely short — merely two months from the knowledge cutoff date of May 1, 2025. While North Korea maintains a state of readiness at the Punggye-ri test site and Kim Jong Un recently issued orders for "full readiness" of the nuclear force in late April 2025, similar rhetoric and physical readiness have been present since at least late 2022 without resulting in a nuclear detonation. North Korea has not conducted a nuclear test since September 2017. Historically, continuous warnings of an 'imminent' test by the U.S. and South Korea, including around the late 2024 U.S. elections, have passed without incident. Furthermore, China's opposition historically acts as a significant constraint. The baseline rate for a test in an active phase is roughly 0.5 per year, but given the 8-year hiatus, the annual probability is likely closer to 10-20%, which translates to a mere 2-3% base rate over a two-month window. Even with a modest risk premium for the heightened rhetoric in April 2025, the chances of a test occurring in this precise 61-day timeframe remain very low.

With a knowledge cutoff of May 1, 2025, there are only two months remaining until the July 1, 2025 resolution date. A Russian land invasion into a new country (such as Moldova, Georgia, or a NATO member state) would require a substantial troop buildup. U.S. intelligence would almost certainly detect and publicly warn about this buildup months in advance, exactly as they did in late 2021 and early 2022 prior to the invasion of Ukraine. Given that there are currently no such reports or warnings of massive troop concentrations on other borders, a new invasion in the next 60 days is highly improbable. Furthermore, multiple European intelligence agencies (such as from Germany, Denmark, and Poland) have projected that Russia's military remains severely degraded and bogged down in Ukraine; they estimate it would take anywhere from a minimum of six months to up to eight years for Russia to reconstitute forces capable of launching a new attack on another state. Given these intelligence timelines and the very short forecast horizon, the likelihood of a new invasion (and a subsequent US accusation) before July 2025 is near zero. I am forecasting at the lowest permitted value (5%).

As of early 2025, Libya remains one of the few African countries that has signed but not ratified the AfCFTA. The country faces severe ongoing political fragmentation and institutional challenges, preventing standard legislative processes needed for ratification. The submitted evidence notes that Libya has not yet even submitted its provisional schedules of tariff concessions, which is a prerequisite for implementation. With only two months remaining between the May 1, 2025 knowledge cutoff and the July 1, 2025 resolution date, and no evidence of ongoing or accelerated legislative momentum, the likelihood of Libya completing the necessary domestic procedures and depositing its instruments of ratification in this very short window is exceptionally low.
